pattern: Each ball's path is independent and fully determined row by row: at cell (row, col) the board value tells you the next column, and the ball only gets stuck when two adjacent boards form a V (an increasing board immediately followed by a decreasing one, or vice versa) or when a board pushes it past an outer wall.
hints: Simulate each of the n balls separately, tracking its current column as it moves down one row at a time.

At row r, column c: the ball moves to column c + grid[r][c]. If that next column is out of bounds, or the board at the next column in the same row faces the opposite way (creating a V), the ball is stuck -- answer -1 for this ball.

Concretely: let nc = c + grid[r][c]. If nc < 0, nc >= n, or grid[r][nc] != grid[r][c], the ball is stuck; otherwise move to nc and continue to the next row. If it survives all rows, its final column is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: statement example one, mixed grid with several V traps
statement example two, single cell stuck against the left wall
statement example three, alternating rows let every ball but the last pass through
single row single column redirecting right hits the wall
single column, tall grid, all boards redirect right so the ball is stuck immediately at the wall
a right board directly followed by a left board on the last column forms a wall-adjacent V
all boards uniform so every ball passes straight through unchanged
a right-left V trap in the middle catches the two balls straddling it, others pass

stress.description: random +-1 grids up to the max size
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 100))
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    grid = [[rng.choice([1, -1]) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [grid]
